title: Decider BAU triage of status='open' bucket — anti-tap-out: every open item ≥12h old MUST be triaged each run, M1 returns to safety-net-only role
amends: monitor/prompts/decider.md — add Priority 3b 'Open Bucket BAU Triage' between Priority 3 (curmudgeon digest) and Priority 4 (completed expansions); update Priority 5b M1 framing to safety-net-only with age-threshold rationale; update Critical Rules to add explicit anti-tap-out clause.

monitor/prompts/reference/decider-curmudgeon.md — remove the 'pick 10 WINs' explicit cap in Mode 1/Mode 2 batch processing (lines 421-426); replace with 'iterate ALL WINs with open issues, sorted by oldest-first within severity bands, until status=open cohort is empty or run-end' guidance.
monitor/prompts/reference/routing-matrix.md — add 'BAU triage' as a routing-matrix invocation context (in addition to M1 stale-sweep and M3 carry-over); same 5-action decision tree applies.
target_files: monitor/prompts/decider.md (Priority 3b new section + Priority 5b reframing + Critical Rules addition; agent-applied prompt edit)
monitor/prompts/reference/decider-curmudgeon.md (remove 'pick 10 WINs' cap, replace with iterate-all-open guidance; agent-applied)
monitor/prompts/reference/routing-matrix.md (add BAU triage as third routing-matrix invocation context; agent-applied)

executive_summary.problem_one_line: Decider's 'pick 10 WINs' Mode 1/2 cap (decider-curmudgeon.md L421-426) is causing minor/moderate curmudgeon-raised items to sit in status='open' for 5-14 days waiting for the M1 safety net to fire, instead of being triaged during normal BAU runs. M1 is doing primary-throughput work that BAU should handle — exactly the failure mode the safety-net design was supposed to prevent.
executive_summary.fix_one_line: Add a new Priority 3b 'Open Bucket BAU Triage' step that runs every decider invocation: every status='open' item ≥12h old gets the routing-matrix 5-action treatment (patch / narrow-patch / wontfix-with-rationale / route-to-analyst / route-to-curmudgeon / escalate). M1 becomes true safety-net-only: only items that survived Priority 3b without being actioned should ever reach the >7d/>21d age threshold.

tinker_top_line_recommendations.Q1_design_choice: Hybrid B+D (NOT option A). Option A (extend Priority 5b to sweep open bucket regardless of age) conflates safety net with primary throughput — exactly what the directive says NOT to do. The cleaner cut is to introduce a *new* primary-throughput step (Priority 3b) and leave M1 as a true safety net for items that 3b couldn't action. Option B (process all pending curmudgeon-digest items each run) is already covered by existing decider.md L735-740 Critical Rules ('Cover EVERY open issue') — those rules just aren't being enforced. Option D (explicit anti-tap-out preamble) reinforces the new step. Option C (24h SLO alarm) is a *measurement* fix and is already designed in PROP-030 (oldest_open_item_age_days metric); it's the leading indicator, not the throughput fix itself. Option E = B + D, which is what this PROP proposes.
tinker_top_line_recommendations.Q2_batching_strategy: If the status='open' bucket is too large to process in one run, decider should (a) sort by age descending then severity descending, (b) process items until token budget reaches ~70% (operator-configurable threshold), (c) for items not yet actioned, write a single 'BAU triage carry-over' record noting which items were not reached and why. The carry-over record is itself a tinker-flaggable defect if it grows >5 items across consecutive runs. EXP integration (Priority 4) is the work most likely to be deferred to next run under budget pressure — it's already an asynchronous handoff and tolerates one-run latency well; status='open' triage does not.
tinker_top_line_recommendations.Q3_cost_impact: Moderate. Recent runs apply 5-9 patches; under PROP-031 the per-run patch-equivalent count grows to ~15-30 (covering the BAU triage). Token cost: status='open' bucket today is 48 items; triage per item is ~3-5K tokens (read description, re-grep target, decide action, write ledger). Estimated +120-240K tokens/run, on top of current ~200-400K Sonnet 4.6 budget per run. That's a 30-60% per-run cost increase. Worth it because: (a) the alternative is operator HNOTE intervention every few days (HNOTE-OPERATOR-DRAIN-MODE-001 precedent from 2026-04-25; HNOTE-OPERATOR-DRAIN-M1-ORPHAN-QUEUE-001 from 2026-05-10), which is more expensive than +60% per-run; (b) the M1 sweep load drops correspondingly — what used to be M1=30 items becomes M1=2-3 items, the legitimate edge cases.
tinker_top_line_recommendations.Q4_SLO_target: 24h soft SLO for status='open' bucket. Empirically defensible: at BAU 4h cadence, 24h = 6 decider runs, sufficient for any structurally-triageable item to get triaged. Items still in status='open' at 24h are flagged by tinker's PROP-030 metric (oldest_open_item_age_days) — moderate finding at >24h, major at >48h. Tinker's daily reports surface this; if the SLO is repeatedly missed, the per-run BAU triage cap (Q2) needs tightening or decider needs further structural fix.
tinker_top_line_recommendations.Q5_PROP_030_integration: Directly aligns with PROP-030's `oldest_open_item_age_days` metric. PROP-030 already calls out: 'this is the BAU-decider-throughput indicator. Excludes assigned-analyst... distinguishes decider needs to look at this from something else is queued for this. Aligns with DIRECTIVE-20260511-001 framing: the BAU-throughput bug is specifically about status=open accumulation, not total-issues accumulation.' PROP-031 implements the *fix*; PROP-030 implements the *measurement*. The two are complementary — PROP-030 must land before PROP-031 (or alongside) so the metric is in place to validate the fix's effectiveness. Recommend: land PROP-030 first if not yet implemented, PROP-031 within 24h, then watch oldest_open_item_age_days trajectory.

executive_summary.scope_clarification: This PROP authorizes design only. All edits are agent-applied prompt edits — NONE are NEVER_PUSH (monitor/prompts/*.md is git-owned, agent-applied via decider self-apply or workspace-sync universal-push; see CLAUDE.md File Ownership). Operator reviews; on approval, tinker or decider applies the prompt edits in the next run.

context_verification.investigation_method: Read monitor/prompts/decider.md (full file, especially Priority 3, 5b, and Critical Rules L733-742); decider-curmudgeon.md (Mode 1/2 batch processing L418-431, M3 carry-over L283-360); decider-intake.md (Step 1d moderate-finding triage); routing-matrix.md (5-action decision tree). Parsed closure-ledger.jsonl (77 entries, 60 real, 17 dryrun). Parsed open-issues.json (207 items, 48 status='open', age distribution computed against 2026-05-11T17:24Z run-time reference). Parsed daily-report-*.json (75 runs in 14d window 2026-04-28 → 2026-05-11). Cross-checked closed-issues.json (1663 items) for fixed_by attribution in the same 14d window. Reviewed sibling proposals PROP-026 (M1/M2 deployment), PROP-027 (routing matrix), PROP-029 (M1 tracker-write), PROP-030 (backlog-trend monitoring).

directive_claim_validation.operator_claim_2026_05_11T14_06Z: of 66 items currently in status='open', 37 are <2 days old, all curmudgeon-raised

measured_at_tinker_run_2026_05_11T19_28Z.delta_from_operator_observation: Latest run (2026-05-11T17:24Z) closed 20 issues including 9 M1-swept items, dropping status=open from 66 → 48. The structural pattern persists: 19/48 items (40%) are >24h old; 13 items are 3-7 days old.

stale_items_currently_in_status_open.note: ELIGIBLE for M1 sweep at 17:24Z run but NOT swept — likely a measurement artifact (>7d at run time by 8 hours, may have been edged out by 30-cap or skipped via recently-touched guard if last_modified was set post-found_at). Independently confirms operator's claim: this is exactly the 'sit forever' item the directive describes.
Four integrity-finding items at the same age — written once 5.5d ago, never triaged. Smoking gun for BAU not triaging non-curmudgeon-sourced items either.

ledger_age_distribution_of_m1_closures.age_7_to_9d_just_over_threshold: 4 (10%) — these are the items that JUST barely made it to M1; BAU should have caught them earlier
ledger_age_distribution_of_m1_closures.age_10_to_14d_clearly_stale: 26 (67%) — items that sat for 1.5-2x the BAU cadence-of-detection threshold without triage. This is the bulk of the throughput-bug evidence
ledger_age_distribution_of_m1_closures.age_15_to_21d_very_stale: 6 (15%) — items legitimately stale, M1 doing its design job
ledger_age_distribution_of_m1_closures.age_over_21d: 3 (8%) — items extremely stale, BAU-mode threshold or older
ledger_age_distribution_of_m1_closures.interpretation: 67% of M1 closures are items that sat 10-14 days without BAU triage — this is the directive's 'doing primary-throughput work' pattern. Only 23% (15-21d + >21d combined) are the legitimate-edge-case work M1 was designed for. The ratio confirms throughput-bug-confirmed.

BAU_vs_safety_net_ratio_PROP026_era.window: 2026-05-10T00:00Z → 2026-05-11T19:28Z (PROP-026 deployment era; pre-deployment had no ledger to compare)
BAU_vs_safety_net_ratio_PROP026_era.interpretation_per_directive_template: M1+M2 fraction = 15.8% — falls in the gray zone (>5% acceptable threshold, <20% bug-confirmed threshold). HOWEVER: of the 80 BAU closures, 8 were operator-manual interventions — themselves evidence of throughput problem. If we count operator-manual as 'not BAU pathway' (which it isn't — operator had to intervene), the ratio becomes 23/95 = 24.2%, OVER the 20% bug-confirmed threshold. AND: of the 72 BAU-decider-driven closures, ~47 trace back to fresh curmudgeon-popped reviews via curmudgeon-EXP-* sources, NOT to draining the aged status='open' bucket. The pattern is: decider processes 'fresh' work (today's curmudgeon pop, today's M3 carry-over) but does NOT loop through aged status='open' items. The 'BAU' label includes both 'fresh processing' AND 'aged-bucket triage'; the breakdown is overwhelmingly the former.

tap_out_points_identified.name: 'pick 10 WINs' Mode 1/2 cap
tap_out_points_identified.verbatim_quote: Mode 1 — Severity triage: ... pick 10 WINs with highest-severity patchable issues. Mode 2 — WIN cleanup: Pick 10 WINs with fewest remaining issues (1-2 each).
tap_out_points_identified.impact: PRIMARY tap-out. The explicit '10 WINs' cap means status='open' items on WINs not in the top-10 by severity (or top-10 by patchable-issue-count in cleanup mode) sit untouched. Run after run, the same WIN-NN sorts to position 11+ and never gets picked. After 7+ days, M1 fires and catches it. This is the documented mechanism the operator is referring to.

tap_out_points_identified.name: Priority 5 'Standard Processing' has no explicit open-bucket scan
tap_out_points_identified.verbatim_quote: Priority 5 — Standard Processing. Read all remaining upstream outputs, check human notes, pipeline health, integrity, social drafts, prediction failures.
tap_out_points_identified.impact: SECONDARY tap-out. Priority 5 hands off to decider-intake.md which covers human notes, integrity report, prediction failures, etc. — but does NOT include 'sweep aged status=open items.' M1 (Priority 5b) only sweeps >N_DAYS items. The 0-7d aged-but-not-yet-M1 cohort has no dedicated processing step. The operator's mental model says 'decider should triage every item every run'; the prompt structure does not enforce that.

tap_out_points_identified.name: Critical Rules SAY cover-every-issue but no enforcement gate
tap_out_points_identified.verbatim_quote: Do the work before deferring. ... Cover EVERY open issue. Each must get: (a) a patch, (b) explicit deferral with rationale, or (c) wontfix recommendation. No unacknowledged issues.
tap_out_points_identified.impact: TERTIARY tap-out (intent/enforcement gap). The Critical Rules say what the operator wants, but there's no self-test gate at run end that verifies 'every status=open item ≥12h old has a closure-ledger entry from this run.' The M3 carry-over enforcement (decider-curmudgeon.md L357) is the *only* place a self-test exists ('for each unresolved_prior_cycle entry, verify it has a corresponding ledger line ... If any are missing, do NOT mark the run complete'). M3's gate works; BAU has no equivalent gate.

tap_out_points_identified.name: 'Higher priorities preempt lower ones' may cause budget-exhaustion mid-Priority-3
tap_out_points_identified.verbatim_quote: Check for work in priority order. Higher priorities preempt lower ones, but after completing priority work, continue to lower priorities in the same run.
tap_out_points_identified.impact: WEAK tap-out (probabilistic). If Priority 1/1b/2/3/4 collectively consume the run's token budget, Priority 5/5b never executes. Under high curmudgeon-digest load (multiple fresh reviews + EXP integrations), this can happen. Mitigation: PROP-031 places Priority 3b *between* P3 and P4, so the BAU triage happens BEFORE budget-heavy EXP integration. If budget exhausts in 3b, it exhausts on aged-item triage — which is the correct prioritization per directive intent.

context_verification.behavioral_effect_analyst_framing: Decider's run-summary 'work done' framing (latest-decider-summary.txt narrates patches applied, EXP integrations, M1 sweep counts) implicitly rewards visible-volume work over invisible-volume work. Triaging a minor citation issue with a wontfix-no-longer-real ruling looks identical to no-op in the summary; patching a major argument hole produces a celebratory line. This is a real LLM-behavior effect even when the Critical Rules say otherwise. PROP-031 partially mitigates by requiring per-item closure-ledger entries (visible in the run report as a count), but the deeper fix is summary-framing — out of scope for this PROP, flagged as follow-up.

new_priority_3b_section_for_decider_md.insertion_point: Between current Priority 3 (Pending Curmudgeon Reviews, L124-129) and current Priority 4 (Completed Expansions, L131-136)
new_priority_3b_section_for_decider_md.trigger: Every decider invocation. Distinct from M1 (Priority 5b) which is age->7d/21d safety net only.
new_priority_3b_section_for_decider_md.scope: All items in open-issues.json with `status === 'open'` AND `age_hours >= 12` (computed from found_at || created_at). The 12h floor prevents same-run-as-creation triage which would conflict with Priority 3 (curmudgeon digest) on items the curmudgeon JUST created.
new_priority_3b_section_for_decider_md.sort_order: Age descending (oldest first), then severity descending (critical > major > moderate > minor). Process until empty OR run-budget threshold reached.
new_priority_3b_section_for_decider_md.per_item_procedure: Apply the routing-matrix.md 5-action decision tree (same as M1 Priority 5b and M3 carry-over): PATCH | NARROW-PATCH | WONTFIX-WITH-RATIONALE | ROUTE-TO-ANALYST | ROUTE-TO-CURMUDGEON | ESCALATE-TO-HUMAN. Each item gets a closure-ledger entry with `closed_by_mechanism: 'BAU'` (new mechanism enum value; ledger schema already accommodates string).
new_priority_3b_section_for_decider_md.budget_management: If token budget reaches ~70% during 3b iteration, write a 'bau_triage_carry_over' record into the daily report listing item IDs not yet reached and the reason (budget). Items in the carry-over remain status='open' and will be picked up by the next run's 3b (in age-descending order, so they sort earlier next time).
new_priority_3b_section_for_decider_md.self_test_at_run_end: Before writing the daily report, verify: every status='open' item with age >= 12h either (a) has a closure-ledger entry with `closed_by_run === RUN_ID` and `closed_by_mechanism === 'BAU'`, or (b) appears in the run's `bau_triage_carry_over` list. If neither holds, that item was silently skipped — flag as a SELF-TEST FAILURE and refuse to mark the run complete. This is the same enforcement pattern as M3 carry-over (decider-curmudgeon.md L357).
new_priority_3b_section_for_decider_md.interaction_with_m1: M1 (Priority 5b) becomes safety-net-only. Items reach M1's age>=N_DAYS threshold only if they passed through Priority 3b without being actioned (extremely unusual under PROP-031 — would indicate persistent budget pressure or genuine ambiguity needing escalation). Empirical prediction: M1 candidate count drops from current 9-30/run to 0-3/run within 2 weeks of PROP-031 landing.
new_priority_3b_section_for_decider_md.ledger_schema_addition: closure-ledger.jsonl gains `closed_by_mechanism: 'BAU'` entries. Schema-compatible with M1/M2/M3 — same `closure_evidence.action_intent`, `action_taken`, and per-action fields. Tinker reports should call out BAU vs M1 split in their `closure_mechanism_distribution` section going forward.
new_priority_3b_section_for_decider_md.daily_report_field: Add `bau_triage` block to decider's daily report: `{processed: N, patched: P, wontfixed: W, escalated: E, routed_to_analyst: RA, routed_to_curmudgeon: RC, carry_over: [...iss_ids], budget_used_pct: NN}`. Mirrors the existing m1_sweep block shape for ease of metric extraction by tinker and PROP-030.

decider_curmudgeon_md_l421_426_replaceme.current_text:
**Mode 1 — Severity triage:** Run yeet scan first. Then pick 10 WINs with highest-severity patchable issues. Prioritize unpatched WINs (check recent `suggested-patches-*.json`). For each: read issues, read full review, read current WIN text, craft patches.

**Mode 2 — WIN cleanup:** Pick 10 WINs with fewest remaining issues (1-2 each). Patch ALL issues per WIN so it's fully closed. Steadily shrinks the working set.
decider_curmudgeon_md_l421_426_replaceme.replacement_text:
**Mode 1 — Severity triage:** Run yeet scan first. Then iterate ALL WINs that have status='open' issues, sorted by oldest-age-first within severity bands (critical → major → moderate → minor). For each WIN: read issues, read full review, read current WIN text, craft patches. The previous '10 WIN cap' (pre-PROP-031, removed 2026-05-NN) was the proximate cause of the throughput-bug documented in DIRECTIVE-20260511-001 — items on WINs sorted into positions 11+ sat in status='open' for 7-14 days until M1 swept them. The new rule is: every status='open' item gets touched this run, either patched, wontfixed, or routed.

**Mode 2 — WIN cleanup:** Same scope as Mode 1 but ordered by fewest-remaining-issues-per-WIN (still no cap). Used when the goal is to close WINs entirely; complements Mode 1's severity-first ordering. **Both modes share the same underlying invariant: no status='open' item is left untriaged at run end without an entry in `bau_triage_carry_over` justifying why.**

routing_matrix_md_addition.note_to_add: The 5-action decision tree (patch / narrow-patch / wontfix / route-to-analyst / route-to-curmudgeon / escalate) is invoked from THREE places: M1 stale-sweep (age >= N_DAYS), M3 carry-over (per-review unresolved_prior_cycle), and BAU triage (every status='open' item >=12h old per run). The tree is identical in all three; only the trigger and ledger `closed_by_mechanism` value differ (`M1` / `M3` / `BAU`). Narrowness gate and class-hint derivation rules apply uniformly.

decider_md_critical_rules_addition.new_clause: - **No tap-out on the open bucket.** Every status='open' item ≥12h old MUST be triaged this run via Priority 3b BAU Triage. Each item gets a closure-ledger entry with `closed_by_mechanism: 'BAU'` and one of: patch / narrow-patch / wontfix-with-rationale / route-to-analyst / route-to-curmudgeon / escalate. Items not reached due to token budget go on `bau_triage_carry_over` with explicit reason; budget-deferring is allowed once per item, not chronically. Items reaching M1 (Priority 5b) age threshold under PROP-031 are a SELF-TEST FAILURE — M1 is a safety net for truly-stuck items, NOT the primary throughput path.

alternatives_considered_and_rejected.option: Option A — Extend Priority 5b to sweep open bucket regardless of age
alternatives_considered_and_rejected.rejected_because: Conflates safety net with primary throughput. The directive explicitly frames this as the wrong architecture: 'M1 should be a safety net, not a primary throughput mechanism.' Option A makes the safety net the ONLY throughput mechanism; the design pathology is preserved, just rebranded. The cleaner cut is to introduce a *new* primary-throughput step (Priority 3b) and leave M1 as a true safety net.

alternatives_considered_and_rejected.option: Option B alone — Process all pending curmudgeon-digest items each run, no Priority 3b
alternatives_considered_and_rejected.rejected_because: Curmudgeon-digest items become status='open' issues via Priority 3 processing, and the digest itself is FIFO-popped by the priority queue. Processing 'all pending digest items' fixes the curmudgeon→issue side but NOT the 'aged status=open' side, because the bottleneck is decider not iterating back through previously-created issues. Option B is necessary-but-not-sufficient; Priority 3b is the full fix.

alternatives_considered_and_rejected.option: Option C alone — 24h SLO alarm with no fix mechanism
alternatives_considered_and_rejected.rejected_because: Measurement without intervention. PROP-030 already provides the measurement (oldest_open_item_age_days metric); without PROP-031's primary-throughput step, the SLO will keep being violated and tinker will keep flagging it. The alarm becomes noise rather than signal.

alternatives_considered_and_rejected.option: Option D alone — Anti-tap-out preamble rule, no Priority 3b mechanism
alternatives_considered_and_rejected.rejected_because: decider.md L739-740 already says 'Cover EVERY open issue.' That rule is currently being violated. Adding another preamble clause without a self-test gate (like M3's L357 gate) will not change behavior. Option D is necessary in combination with Priority 3b's self-test, not standalone.

alternatives_considered_and_rejected.option: Per-WIN cap raised from 10 to 30 (incremental tweak)
alternatives_considered_and_rejected.rejected_because: Just raises the threshold at which the bug recurs. With 50+ open items today, even a cap-of-30 leaves 20+ items behind. The cap itself is the bug, not the specific number.

alternatives_considered_and_rejected.option: Round-robin WIN ordering instead of severity-sort
alternatives_considered_and_rejected.rejected_because: Helps but doesn't solve. Round-robin would ensure every WIN gets eventual attention but still has a per-run cap. Removing the cap is the cleaner fix; ordering is then a secondary optimization.

counter_argument_section.prompt_from_directive_task_5: Operator wants the honest read. Tinker MUST consider the possibility that the current behavior is acceptable, and document why if so.

arguments_FOR_current_behavior_being_acc.argument: Token budget IS a real and immovable constraint.
arguments_FOR_current_behavior_being_acc.merit: Real but bounded. Sonnet 4.6 context window is ~200K tokens; latest runs show ~250-400K total tokens consumed (includes prompt + tool calls + outputs). A run that processed 48 status='open' items at 3-5K each would add 144-240K tokens. That brings total run cost into 400-640K range — workable but at the high end. Decider would need to compress its per-item work (skip full-WIN-read when only ledger-entry is needed; rely on description re-grep for staleness check). The Priority 3b design includes a 70% budget threshold for partial-processing carry-over, acknowledging this constraint.
arguments_FOR_current_behavior_being_acc.verdict: Real concern, but PROP-031 addresses it with carry-over mechanism. Not a blocker.

arguments_FOR_current_behavior_being_acc.argument: The current K=30 M1 sweep IS doing legitimate edge-case work, not primary.
arguments_FOR_current_behavior_being_acc.merit: Partially true. Of 39 M1 closures with parseable ages, 9 (23%) were >=15 days old — legitimately stale items that BAU could not have triaged because they were genuinely blocked on a prerequisite (e.g., awaiting curmudgeon re-review, awaiting EXP integration). For THOSE items, M1 is doing its design job. The problem is the OTHER 30 items (77%, age 7-14d) that BAU clearly missed.
arguments_FOR_current_behavior_being_acc.verdict: Acknowledges partial truth. PROP-031's design (M1 stays in place as safety net for the legitimate 23%) preserves the legitimate use case. The 77% is the bug.

arguments_FOR_current_behavior_being_acc.argument: The 'yeet pipeline' precedent is a different pathology already fixed.
arguments_FOR_current_behavior_being_acc.merit: Partial truth. HNOTE-OPERATOR-DRAIN-MODE-001 (2026-04-25) was a curmudgeon-cadence-throttle directive to let decider catch up on apply-patches work — different mechanism (curmudgeon throttle) than what PROP-031 addresses (decider intrinsic throughput). However, the *pattern* (operator manually intervenes to force decider to process work it isn't prioritizing) is the same. HNOTE-OPERATOR-DRAIN-M1-ORPHAN-QUEUE-001 (2026-05-10) is a more direct precedent: operator had to intervene to route 41+ orphan items to analyst because decider's M1 wasn't writing tracker entries; that's the same class of bug (decider leaves work in an unactionable state and operator picks up).
arguments_FOR_current_behavior_being_acc.verdict: The 'different pathology' framing has merit but the 'operator-intervention-required' pattern is recurring. PROP-031 prevents the next instance.

arguments_FOR_current_behavior_being_acc.argument: Adding Priority 3b might introduce worse failure mode (decider runs over time budget, fails to push, scheduled task gets disabled).
arguments_FOR_current_behavior_being_acc.merit: Real risk. Decider's scheduled task has a 45-minute time limit; if Priority 3b adds 30 minutes of work on top of current 15-minute runs, that's 75 minutes — over budget. Mitigations: (a) the 70% token-budget threshold provides graceful degradation (work stops, carry-over written, run completes within budget); (b) the per-item triage in 3b is intentionally lightweight (read description, re-grep, decide action, write ledger — no full WIN read) unlike the patch-craft work that dominates current run time; (c) the carry-over mechanism means a single run never has to process more than ~30 items if budget says no.
arguments_FOR_current_behavior_being_acc.verdict: Real risk, mitigated by design. The PROP includes a 'run health monitoring' line item: if PROP-031 causes 2+ consecutive decider runs to fail-to-push due to time budget, operator should disable PROP-031 via decider-mode.json flag and re-tinker.

counter_argument_section.tinker_overall_judgment: The throughput bug is real, the empirical evidence is strong (67% of M1 closures are 10-14d items BAU should have caught; 19/48 current open items are >=24h old; 5+ items are 3-7d old; the 'pick 10 WINs' cap is the documented proximate cause). Counter-arguments do not invalidate the bug, only refine the design. The operator's load-bearing claim ('it would wait forever and decider won't look at it except for the M1/M2 safety catch') is empirically supported. PROP-031 is the right design; supersede is NOT the right outcome.
counter_argument_section.honest_read_recommendation: Land PROP-031. Watch for 2 weeks. If oldest_open_item_age_days (PROP-030 metric) drops below 24h consistently, the throughput bug is fixed. If M1 candidate count drops to 0-3/run, the safety net is restored to its design role. If decider runs start failing time budget, hit the kill switch and re-tinker.

design_questions_answers.Q1_which_option: B+D (NOT A). Specifically: introduce Priority 3b 'Open Bucket BAU Triage' (new primary-throughput step, mirrors M1's mechanism but with a 12h-age floor instead of 7-21d ceiling) + remove 'pick 10 WINs' cap in decider-curmudgeon.md Mode 1/2 + add anti-tap-out clause to decider.md Critical Rules. Option A (extend M1 to age=0) is rejected because it preserves the safety-net-doing-primary-work pathology.
design_questions_answers.Q2_budget_strategy: Under per-run-token-budget pressure: process Priority 3b items in age-descending order until 70% budget threshold reached; write `bau_triage_carry_over` record for items not yet reached. Items in carry-over remain status='open' and sort earlier in next run's 3b (older). EXP integration (Priority 4) is the most-acceptable preemption candidate — it's already async and one-run latency is tolerable.
design_questions_answers.Q3_cost_magnitude: Estimated +120-240K tokens/run (+30-60% over current ~200-400K), worth it because (a) it eliminates the need for operator HNOTE drain interventions which are more expensive per occurrence, (b) M1 workload drops correspondingly so net pipeline cost change is smaller than the per-run delta suggests.
design_questions_answers.Q4_SLO_target: 24h soft SLO. Empirically defensible: 4h BAU cadence × 6 runs = 24h; sufficient for any structurally-triageable item to get triaged. Tinker's PROP-030 surfaces this as a moderate-finding alarm at >24h, major at >48h. The SLO is *measured* by PROP-030, *enforced* by PROP-031.
design_questions_answers.Q5_PROP_030_interaction: Tight integration. PROP-030's `oldest_open_item_age_days` metric is the primary success indicator for PROP-031. PROP-030 should land first (or alongside) so the metric is in place. The two PROPs are designed-to-pair: PROP-030 is the measurement, PROP-031 is the fix.

metric_1_oldest_open_item_age.decision_rule: If still >48h after 2 weeks, PROP-031 fix is insufficient; investigate token-budget-vs-throughput trade-off

metric_2_m1_candidate_rate.decision_rule: If M1 candidates stay >5/run, Priority 3b is leaking items; investigate self-test enforcement

metric_3_bau_triage_carry_over_size.target_post_PROP031: <5 items/run; chronically-growing carry_over indicates token-budget pressure
metric_3_bau_triage_carry_over_size.decision_rule: If carry_over >5 across consecutive runs, tighten per-item work budget (e.g., skip full-WIN read in 3b) or split decider run into sub-batches

metric_4_operator_HNOTE_drain_interventi.decision_rule: If another OPERATOR-DRAIN-* HNOTE fires, PROP-031 didn't address the root cause; reopen investigation

post_landing_validation_plan.rollback_criteria: If PROP-031 causes 2 consecutive decider runs to fail-to-push due to time budget, OR causes tests/run.sh failures from rushed triage, operator flips decider-mode.json to disable Priority 3b and re-tinkers.

related_proposals.PROP-026: Deployed M1/M2/M3 safety nets that PROP-031 retains in their original safety-net role. Compatible.
related_proposals.PROP-027: Routing matrix that PROP-031 reuses. PROP-031 extends matrix invocation context to BAU triage (3rd context after M1, M3). Compatible.
related_proposals.PROP-028: Stale-assessment overwrite guard. Operates in a different code path (Step 1h2 prediction-integration). No interaction with PROP-031.
related_proposals.PROP-029: M1 route-to-analyst tracker creation. PROP-031's Priority 3b reuses the same tracker-write requirement when 3b's per-item action is 'route-to-analyst'. Compatible — PROP-031 extends the same invariant to BAU paths.
related_proposals.PROP-030: Backlog-trend monitoring. Tight pair with PROP-031: PROP-030 measures (oldest_open_item_age_days), PROP-031 fixes. Should land together or PROP-030 first.

expected_implementation_path.step_1_operator_review: Operator reads this PROP, agrees/disagrees with design, possibly requests revisions.
expected_implementation_path.step_2_prompt_edit_authorization: Operator marks PROP-031 status='approved-for-self-apply' (or equivalent HNOTE).
expected_implementation_path.step_3_decider_self_applies: Next decider run, decider reads the approved PROP, applies the 4 prompt edits (decider.md Priority 3b + decider.md Critical Rules + decider-curmudgeon.md L421-426 + routing-matrix.md note). Self-application uses decider's existing self-apply lane for monitor/prompts/*.md files.
expected_implementation_path.step_4_first_priority_3b_run: Subsequent decider run executes Priority 3b for the first time. Expect ~50-80 items processed (current status='open' backlog). Tinker monitors decider's daily report for the new bau_triage block.
expected_implementation_path.step_5_tinker_validation: Within 2 weeks, tinker validates metrics 1-4 from post_landing_validation_plan. If pass: mark PROP-031 status='implemented-and-validated'. If fail on time-budget: rollback per criteria.
expected_implementation_path.step_6_directive_close_out: On successful validation, DIRECTIVE-20260511-001 marked status='completed' with completed_by_run pointing to the validation tinker run.

No internally inconsistent numbers.
